name: Gangesha Upadhyaya
dates_notes: Mithilā Naiyāyika; dates fixed by Bhattacharya (1958, History of Navya-Nyāya in Mithilā) and Matilal (1968, The Navya-Nyāya Doctrine of Negation) to the late thirteenth and early fourteenth century. The Tattva-Cintāmaṇi's terminus ante quem is given by its citation in Vardhamāna Upādhyāya's Prakāśa.

lineage_polemical.note: The entire Tattva-Cintāmaṇi pramāṇa-vāda apparatus is built in dialogue with Dharmakīrti: the Navya-Nyāya formal vyāpti, the analysis of avacchedaka, the treatment of paryāpti — each is developed as the Naiyāyika tool for answering the technical Buddhist epistemology of the Pramāṇa-Vārttika and Pramāṇa-Viniścaya. The Vyāpti-Pañcaka's stepwise refinement is calibrated against the Buddhist counterexamples that Old-Nyāya could no longer absorb.

Through the inherited Old-Nyāya stack the Tattva-Cintāmaṇi continues the engagement with Dignāga: the sāmānya-vāda section of the Anumāna-Khaṇḍa is the Navya-Nyāya defence of jāti-realism against the apoha-vāda.

Yaśovijaya studied Navya-Nyāya at Vārāṇasī in the technical idiom Gaṅgeśa established and engages the Tattva-Cintāmaṇi apparatus directly in his Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā and Nyāya-Loka; the Jaina-Navya-Nyāya synthesis Yaśovijaya produces refutes Naiyāyika positive theses (paramāṇu-cosmology, jāti-realism, the four-pramāṇa scheme) using the Naiyāyikas' own technical apparatus.

core_thesis: Gaṅgeśa's *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi* founds Navya-Nyāya — the 'New Logic' — by reformulating the entire *pramāṇa*-machinery of Old-Nyāya in a technical idiom precise enough to defeat the late-twelfth-century Buddhist and Advaitin pressure (Śrīharṣa, *Khaṇḍana-Khaṇḍa-Khādya* 1) that Old-Nyāya could no longer absorb. Three innovations define the system. First, *vyāpti* (invariable concomitance) is reformulated in the *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*'s *Vyāpti-Pañcaka* section. Where Uddyotakara (*Nyāya-Vārttika* on NS 1.1.5) and Udayana relied on a *tri-rūpa-liṅga* characterisation, Gaṅgeśa develops five successively more rigorous candidate-definitions in the *Vyāpti-Pañcaka* (*Tattva-Cintāmaṇi*, *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *vyāpti-pañcaka-prakaraṇa*) — each tested against canonical counter-examples and culminating in the *Siddhānta-Lakṣaṇa*. The standard Navya definition (*pratiyogi-tāvacchedaka-sambandhāvacchinna-pratiyogitāka-bhāva-pratiyogi-tāvacchedaka-rūpeṇa hetu-tāvacchedakāvacchinna-niyamaḥ*) is the form the school inherits. Second, *avacchedaka* (the delimiting condition) is given systematic treatment in the *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*'s *avacchedakatva-prakaraṇa*: every property has its mode of being-a-property, every relation its mode of being-the-relation; this lets every inference and every cognition be specified without remainder. Third, *paryāpti* (the exhausting predication relation) is introduced (*Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *paryāpti-vāda*) for cases where a property is located in something via the property's exhaustion of its locus, distinguishing cases where two-ness is in two horses-jointly from cases where horse-ness is in each horse separately. The technical apparatus is the dialectical lingua franca for late-medieval Indian philosophy: Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi*, Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*, Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's *Dīdhiti*, and the entire Navadvīpa scholasticism are unintelligible without it.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Cintamani
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by terminus ante quem of Vardhamāna Upādhyāya's Prakāśa.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the founding text of Navya-Nyāya.
engaged_works.summary: Independent treatise organised by *pramāṇa* into four *khaṇḍas*: *Pratyakṣa-Khaṇḍa*, *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *Upamāna-Khaṇḍa*, *Śabda-Khaṇḍa*. Each *khaṇḍa* opens with a *maṅgala* establishing the methodological standard, advances through systematic *pūrvapakṣa*-and-*uttarapakṣa* on every disputed sub-question, and concludes with a constructive Navya-Nyāya formulation. The *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa* is the longest and the most influential. Its first section (*Tattva-Cintāmaṇi*, *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *vyāpti-pañcaka-prakaraṇa*) gives the five candidate definitions of *vyāpti* and the rigorous tests by which the standard definition is selected; the *avacchedakatva-prakaraṇa* establishes the technical idiom that the entire later school presupposes; the *paryāpti-vāda* introduces the relation that distinguishes joint-locating from distributive-locating predications. The *Pratyakṣa-Khaṇḍa* refines the perceptual analysis (in the *pratyakṣa-lakṣaṇa-prakaraṇa*) to handle Advaitin pressure on the *adhyāsa*-doctrine (against Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* preamble) and Buddhist pressure on *nirvikalpaka* perception. The *Śabda-Khaṇḍa* defends *śābda*-bodha on a Naiyāyika realist basis against the Mīmāṃsā analysis of *anvitābhidhāna* (Prabhākara, *Bṛhatī* on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.24–25). The *Cintāmaṇi* is the most-commented text in late-medieval Sanskrit philosophy; Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's *Dīdhiti* and Mathurānātha's *Māthurī* are the standard Navadvīpa sub-commentaries.
